_meta.scope: Geologic subsurface CO2 sequestration capacity (supercritical CO2 in saline aquifers + depleted oil/gas fields). Excludes mineralization/basalt pathways except where noted as alt estimates.
known_access_failures: Kearns et al. 2017 (Energy Procedia 114:4697-4709, doi:10.1016/j.egypro.2017.03.1603) primary per-region table could NOT be extracted: ScienceDirect and ResearchGate both blocked with CAPTCHA/security-check pages across multiple fetch attempts (direct WebFetch and r.jina.ai proxy). Only the global 8,000-55,000 Gt headline figure and fragmentary secondary-cited regional numbers (North America 2,600-21,770 Gt; Asia ~2,964-2,984 Gt; South America ~2,030 Gt, via a 2025 CCUS-policy review citing Kearns) were recoverable. Treat the 'regions' array below as LOW CONFIDENCE / illustrative, not a faithful reproduction of Kearns Table 3.

global.notes: These three numbers are NOT a single confidence interval on one quantity -- they are anchors from three different, non-comparable methodological tiers, presented together because each is the most-cited figure in its tier: (1) low = 'prudent' risk-screened limit (Gasser/Chen et al., Nature 2025, central 1,460 Gt, range 1,290-2,710 Gt) after applying depth/seismic/protected-area/transboundary exclusion layers to a starting physical potential of ~11,800 Gt; (2) central = CSRC/OGCI Cycle 4/5 aggregated Stored+Commercial+Sub-Commercial+Undiscovered resource across ~54-111 assessed countries (~14,300 Gt), which -- reassuringly -- lands close to the low end of Kearns et al. 2017's independently-derived 8,000-55,000 Gt 'practically accessible' range; (3) high = Kearns et al. 2017 upper bound of practically accessible theoretical capacity worldwide. The spread illustrates that 'global CO2 storage capacity' answers differ by >1 order of magnitude depending on how aggressively non-technical risk/access constraints are applied, not because the underlying geology is disputed.
sources: OGCI CSRC Cycle 4 Main Report (Aug 2024) and Cycle 5 Main Report (Aug 2025), https://www.ogci.com

Kearns, Teletzke, Palmer, Thomann, Kheshgi, Chen, Paltsev, Herzog (2017), 'Developing a Consistent Database for Regional Geologic CO2 Storage Capacity Worldwide', Energy Procedia 114:4697-4709, doi:10.1016/j.egypro.2017.03.1603
Gasser et al. (2025), 'A prudent planetary limit for geologic carbon storage', Nature 645:124-, doi:10.1038/s41586-025-09423-y, PMC12408384

regions.source: Kearns et al. 2017 via secondary citation (primary table inaccessible); cross-check: USGS 2013 US technical ~3,000 Gt + Canada academic ~877 Gt theoretical, see sedimentary_us_canada_tiers.md

Kearns et al. 2017 via secondary citation -- NOTE: this narrow band is very likely a garbled/partial secondary quotation, not the true Kearns Asia range (China alone has academic theoretical estimates of 2,420-3,067 Gt, implying the true Kearns Asia band must be wider). Do not treat as reliable.
regions.confidence: very low - flagged as probably erroneous

regions.source: Kearns et al. 2017 via secondary citation, single-point figure only (no low/high split recovered)

regions.tier: theoretical (deep saline 69 Gt + depleted HC fields 17 Gt + coal beds 1 Gt = 87 Gt total)
regions.source: EU GeoCapacity project final report (Vangkilde-Pedersen et al. 2009, FP6 WP2 D16)
regions.confidence: medium - project-reported total, country breakdown only partially recovered (see countries array and methodology md)

regions.region: Sub-Saharan Africa (assessed countries only: ZAF, MOZ, NGA)
regions.tier: mixed (CSRC aggregated for ZAF/MOZ; IFC 2025 prospective atlas for NGA)
regions.source: CSRC Cycle 4; IFC Nigerian CO2 Storage Atlas (March 2025)
regions.confidence: low - most African basins still unassessed at any tier

countries.name: United States
countries.preferred_source: Teletzke et al. 2018 (ExxonMobil, GHGT-14), saline-only onshore practicable P50
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Gulf Coast dominates every tier: 59% of USGS technical, 72% of Teletzke practicable.
countries.notes: Practicable (Teletzke) chosen as preferred because it is the most conservative, deployment-relevant tier with transparent basin attribution and is the number most used in DAC/CCS siting literature. See sedimentary_us_canada_tiers.md for full tier ladder; do not sum tiers.

countries.name: Canada

alt_estimates.source: Secondary summary of ScienceDirect S0959652623010594, unverified vs Bachu primary
Intl CCS Knowledge Centre, 'Canada's CO2 Landscape' 2021

countries.notes: CSRC preferred for cross-country comparability; national theoretical figure ~6x higher reflects much looser basin-scale criteria.

countries.name: Mexico
key_basins: Gulf of Mexico basin (Burgos, Veracruz, Sureste)
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Mostly Sub-Commercial tier (89.5 Gt), suggesting discovered-but-immature structures.
countries.notes: Cycle 5 country chapter not extracted (PDF truncation); Cycle 4 used. No independent academic cross-check found.

countries.name: Brazil
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: CSRC figure is 100% depleted O&G fields, regulatorily inaccessible; Brazilian Atlas of CO2 (Ketzer et al. 2015) and later saline-aquifer screening studies (Paraná, Santos, Espírito Santo basins) exist but no consolidated national Gt total was recoverable.
countries.notes: Large known gap: Brazil's saline-aquifer theoretical potential (esp. Paraná Basin, one of the largest sedimentary basins in South America) is almost certainly far larger than the 2.47 Gt O&G-only CSRC figure, but no citable national aggregate was found in this pass. Flagged in methodology md as priority follow-up.

countries.name: Argentina
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Neuquén Basin is Argentina's largest hydrocarbon basin (45% of oil, 61% of gas production) and a plausible large saline-aquifer/depleted-field storage target, but no published Gt-scale capacity assessment (national or basin) was located.
countries.notes: Data gap -- absent from CSRC Cycles 2/4/5 and from all academic searches in this pass. Left null rather than fabricated.

countries.name: Norway

alt_estimates.tier: technical (NPD/Sodir CO2 Storage Atlas of the Norwegian Continental Shelf)
alt_estimates.source: Norwegian Offshore Directorate (NPD/Sodir), CO2 Storage Atlas 2014, updated ~2023-2024 to 70-80 Gt (43 Gt saline aquifers + 27 Gt petroleum-related fields per NPD evaluation)

countries.notes: IMPORTANT DISCREPANCY: CSRC revised Norway DOWN from 84.3 Gt (Cycle 4) to 18.1 Gt (Cycle 5) by applying stricter SRMS maturity criteria, while Norway's own regulator (NPD/Sodir) reports a much larger 70-80 Gt technical estimate across the full shelf. CSRC preferred here for cross-country consistency, but the NPD atlas figure is arguably the more authoritative *national* number and should be used if the map is Norway-specific rather than comparative.

countries.name: United Kingdom

alt_estimates.source: CO2Stored / UKSAP, British Geological Survey & The Crown Estate, ~500 offshore storage units across Southern/Central/Northern North Sea + East Irish Sea

countries.notes: CSRC and CO2Stored are broadly consistent (55 vs 78 Gt, same order of magnitude), unlike the Norway case.

countries.name: Germany

alt_estimates.source: GEUS EU CO2 storage summary 2021, citing CO2StoP

countries.onshore_offshore_notes: 100% saline per CSRC; note Germany's federal moratorium/opt-out framework has historically limited onshore storage regardless of geological capacity.
countries.notes: Large upward revision Cycle 4->5 (51->121 Gt) reflects newly assessed saline structures, opposite direction from Norway's revision.

countries.name: Netherlands

alt_estimates.tier: theoretical (CO2StoP 2015: 1.4 Gt saline + 10 Gt HC fields)

key_basins: Southern North Sea (depleted gas fields, e.g., Groningen-adjacent structures)
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Depleted gas fields (offshore) are the dominant near-term target (Porthos, Aramis projects use this).
countries.notes: Not re-covered in CSRC Cycle 5 Europe appendix as fetched; Cycle 4 used.

countries.name: Denmark
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Mix onshore/offshore; Project Greensand (offshore) is the active flagship.
countries.notes: Stable figure across CSRC cycles.

countries.name: France
countries.notes: CO2StoP theoretical (29 Gt) is 4x CSRC's discovered/undiscovered aggregate -- typical theoretical-vs-mature-resource gap.

countries.name: Poland

alt_estimates.source: GEUS EU CO2 storage summary 2021, citing dedicated Polish CO2StoP paper (Poland has one of the largest theoretical-to-mature gaps in Europe)

countries.notes: ~60x gap between CSRC mature-resource figure and CO2StoP theoretical saline estimate -- Poland has extensive unassessed saline formations.

alt_estimates.tier: theoretical (CO2StoP 2015: 5 Gt saline + 134 Mt HC fields)

countries.name: Austria
countries.notes: Largest CSRC-vs-CO2StoP ratio in the European set (~140x) -- Austria's mature/discovered resource base is minuscule relative to theoretical saline potential.

countries.name: Sweden
countries.notes: CSRC reports zero assessed capacity; not geologically implausible but unassessed at CSRC standard.

countries.name: Finland
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Crystalline shield, limited sedimentary basin area -- genuinely low prospectivity, unlike Sweden's data gap.

countries.name: China
countries.preferred_source: CSRC Cycle 5 (2025): 72 sites, ≥21 basins; gas 5.2 Gt + oil/EOR 4.8 Gt, remainder saline

alt_estimates.tier: theoretical saline (27 basins, full brine dissolution assumption)

alt_estimates.source: Wuhan Institute of Rock and Soil Mechanics, CAS (Tarim, Songliao, Bohai Bay, Pearl River Estuary basins dominant)

alt_estimates.tier: effective, upper bound of 65-1551 Gt range
alt_estimates.source: secondary review of Chinese effective-capacity studies

countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Mix onshore/offshore; offshore saline aquifers increasingly studied separately.
countries.notes: Remarkable convergence: CSRC's independently-derived 3,077 Gt aggregate matches Dahowski's theoretical 3,067 Gt almost exactly, lending confidence to the ~3,000 Gt order of magnitude for China despite very different methodologies.

countries.name: India

alt_estimates.tier: theoretical (saline 291 Gt + basalt 97 Gt, low end)
alt_estimates.source: Vishal et al. 2021, systematic capacity assessment

alt_estimates.tier: theoretical (saline 291 Gt + basalt 316 Gt, high end)

countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Basalt-hosted mineralization potential (Deccan Traps) is a distinct pathway from clastic/carbonate saline storage; academic alt-estimate mixes the two.
countries.notes: CSRC preferred as mappable saline+HC figure; basalt component in academic alt is a different storage mechanism (in-situ mineralization) and shouldn't be summed with saline totals without caveats.

countries.name: Indonesia

alt_estimates.tier: theoretical mid (saline 379 Gt + gas reservoirs 6.2 Gt)
theoretical (MEMR estimate: saline 573 Gt + depleted fields 4.8 Gt)
alt_estimates.source: Indonesia Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources

countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Government (MEMR) theoretical figure is ~36x CSRC's mature-resource figure -- one of the largest theoretical/mature gaps in the dataset, reflecting Indonesia's huge unassessed basin area.

countries.name: Japan
countries.preferred_source: CSRC Cycle 5 (2025); depleted fields ~3.5 Gt of total
key_basins: Tomakomai area (offshore Hokkaido, active injection project)

countries.name: Kazakhstan
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Onshore, pre-salt carbonate platforms and post-salt clastics.
countries.notes: Strong cross-validation: CSRC (582.88 Gt) and independent academic effective-capacity estimate (583 Gt) agree almost exactly -- one of the best-corroborated country figures in this dataset.

countries.name: Bangladesh
countries.preferred_source: CSRC Cycle 5 (2025): 20 Gt saline undiscovered + 1.13 Gt gas fields discovered

countries.name: Russia

alt_estimates.tier: CO2-EOR suitable subset only, NOT a national theoretical estimate

countries.onshore_offshore_notes: West Siberian Basin alone is one of the largest sedimentary basins on Earth; by analogy with Kazakhstan's adjacent Precaspian Basin (~460-583 Gt) Russia's true national theoretical potential is very likely in the hundreds to low thousands of Gt, but no citable national assessment was found.
countries.notes: Largest single data gap in this dataset for a G20/major-emitter economy. Absent from CSRC Cycles 2/4/5 entirely. Shogenova et al. 2011 (cited secondarily) covered only NW Russia and explicitly declined to estimate a national figure for data-quality reasons. Flagged as top follow-up priority.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
countries.preferred_source: first comprehensive national assessment of Saudi sedimentary basins (ScienceDirect S0012825223002283), effective capacity in deep saline aquifers ~432 Gt P50, total sedimentary formations ~446 Gt

alt_estimates.tier: aggregated, single oil field only (CSRC SRMS, Cycle 5)
alt_estimates.source: CSRC Cycle 5 -- explicitly not representative, covers 1 discovered oil field, inaccessible

countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Mostly onshore, eastern basins most prospective; western basins (Red Sea side) less favorable except Umm Luj/Yanbu/Jeddah.
countries.notes: Academic estimate strongly preferred over CSRC here because the CSRC figure is known to cover only a single legacy oil field and is not a national assessment (CSRC itself flags Saudi as effectively unassessed at scale).

countries.name: United Arab Emirates
countries.preferred_source: CSRC Cycle 5 (2025): Rub al Khali basin, 5.91 Gt project-specified

countries.name: Algeria
countries.preferred_source: CO2-storage assessment and effective capacity in Algeria (PMC4940307), national range 90 Mt (structural traps, low) to 45 Gt (hydrodynamic traps, high)

alt_estimates.tier: theoretical, extreme upper bound cited for saline aquifers used at In Salah
alt_estimates.source: In Salah-area saline aquifer literature range 320-10,000 Gt -- treat as not credible/too wide to be useful
multiple structural CO2 storage estimates for Algeria's most prospective basin

key_basins: In Salah / Krechba (operating since 2004, >3.8 Mt stored)
countries.notes: Not in CSRC. 45 Gt (hydrodynamic-trap upper national estimate) chosen as preferred; the 10,000 Gt figure sometimes quoted for In Salah-area aquifers is an extreme theoretical outlier and is not used as preferred.

countries.name: South Africa
countries.preferred_source: CSRC Cycle 4 (2024): mostly saline, Sub-Comm 19.91 + Undiscovered 323.02

countries.name: Mozambique
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Depleted-field potential unquantified (large gas discoveries in Rovuma Basin plausibly add meaningfully once mature).

countries.name: Nigeria
countries.preferred_source: IFC Nigerian CO2 Storage Atlas, March 2025 -- saline aquifers and depleted oil/gas fields, Miocene-age Niger Delta formations dominant

alt_estimates.source: CSRC Cycle 4: 'no published information' -- superseded by the March 2025 IFC atlas, which post-dates CSRC Cycle 4's Nigeria assessment window

countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Niger Delta saline aquifers and depleted gas fields identified as most viable; Lagos/Port Harcourt/Warri flagged as CCS-hub-suitable given existing O&G transport infrastructure.
countries.notes: CSRC's '0' reflected a genuine data gap at the time (Cycle 4, Aug 2024), closed by the IFC atlas published March 2025. 10,700 Gt is a prospective/theoretical-tier figure (analogous to CSRC 'Undiscovered'), not a mature resource number, and should be treated with the same caveats as other basin-scale prospective estimates -- but it is the best available and most recent national assessment. Flag for reconciliation once CSRC Cycle 6 potentially incorporates it.

countries.name: Australia
countries.preferred_source: CSRC Cycle 5 (2025): saline basin-play Undiscovered ~473 Gt + Capacity 0.1 + Stored 0.009

alt_estimates.tier: national ('potential'), older Carbon Storage Taskforce lineage

alt_estimates.source: Carbon Storage Taskforce lineage figure cited in CSRC
OGCI CSRC characterization of Australia's commercially-relevant subset

key_basins: Gippsland Basin (offshore Victoria, highest quality/largest capacity)
countries.onshore_offshore_notes: Best reservoirs are offshore (Gippsland); Cooper/Eromanga and Surat are the main onshore targets.
countries.notes: CSRC 485 Gt saline figure is the most current and methodologically transparent; used as preferred despite being far above the 31 Gt 'realistic near-term' subset, which is kept as an alt for anyone mapping deployable-by-2035 capacity specifically.

countries.name: New Zealand
countries.preferred_source: CSRC Cycle 5-lineage: depleted fields ~0.42 Gt (Maui 0.30, Kapuni 0.10, McKee 0.023) + saline ~2.47 Gt (3 formations @1% storage efficiency)
countries.notes: 1% storage efficiency assumption for saline component is conservative relative to typical 2-4% used elsewhere; true saline capacity could be several-fold higher.
